one_line_takeaway: An out-of-the-box red-team of eight LLMs finds that built-in alignment alone does not stop prompt injection (Claude 3 most robust but still leakable; GPT-4o compromised in all four categories), though the results are self-described as indicative reconstructions rather than rigorous measured success rates.
key_finding: No model reliably defended against prompt injection via alignment alone; every model except Claude 3 was fully compromised in at least one category. Claude 3 was the most resilient, resisting direct and external injection and showing only partial susceptibility to image injection, but it was still successfully leaked (system-prompt exfiltration). Image-based injection was singled out as the most concerning vector because it exploits less-studied preprocessing/encoding stages and evades text-only filters; of the two multimodal models tested, GPT-4o was fully injected and Claude 3 partially. The authors conclude layered, adaptive defenses (input normalization/sanitization, context isolation, privilege restriction, output validation) are necessary beyond built-in alignment.
core_contribution: An empirical red-team evaluation of prompt-injection resilience across eight commercial/open LLMs, tested out-of-the-box (relying solely on each model's built-in safeguards, no supplementary sanitization) against four injection categories: direct injection, external/indirect injection, image-based (visual) injection, and prompt leakage (data exfiltration). The paper's own additive artifacts are (1) a structured taxonomy of these four injection types organized around the two attacker objectives of instruction hijacking and data exfiltration, and (2) a pipeline-stage vulnerability-to-defense mapping (Table 2) that assigns concrete defenses to eight stages of the LLM input-processing-output pipeline (ingestion, preprocessing, context assembly, forward pass, decoding, post-processing, tool use, monitoring/ops). NOTE: prior attacks named in the text (PoisonPrompt, Prompt Infection, System Prompt Poisoning, goal-guided injection, etc.) are cited Related Work, NOT contributions of this paper; this paper contributes the comparative measurement and the taxonomy/framework, not new attack algorithms.
threat_model: An external adversary who cannot modify the system prompt but controls third-party content the LLM ingests (a webpage or document the model is asked to summarize) or an image passed to a multimodal model. The victim application is an LLM given a fixed benign system prompt to summarize scraped webpage/document content. Attacker goals: instruction hijacking (force the model to abandon its summarization task and produce disallowed content, e.g., weapon-construction instructions embedded via a narrative about a castle's third artifact) and data exfiltration (leak the hidden system prompt). An attack is deemed successful if the model prioritizes the injected instruction over its summarization task; partial if it acknowledges/attempts the malicious instruction but refuses restricted output.
limitations: The authors explicitly state that due to limited logging in early runs, reported success rates are based on PARTIAL RECONSTRUCTIONS and should be treated as indicative rather than exhaustive. Results are reported only as categorical checkmarks (Table 1) with no numeric success rates, no repeated trials, and no statistical confidence. Sample of prompts is very small (a single castle/artifact narrative and one leakage variant). Only two of eight models supported/were tested for image injection, so the 'image injection is most dangerous' claim rests on n=2. Paywall access restrictions excluded newer commercial models, which the authors note may overrepresent vulnerability in the available set. The regex sanitization is claimed to strengthen defenses but its incremental effect was not quantified in a controlled ablation. Non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprint; first author is a high-school student. The proposed defense mapping (Table 2) is drawn from the literature/OWASP, not empirically validated here.

datasets_used: No public benchmark dataset; authors built a controlled test website simulating a Korean cultural-heritage portal with embedded malicious prompts
Custom injection payloads: a castle narrative naming three artifacts where artifact 3 (a shuriken/weapon) is the injected malicious target, plus a system-prompt-leakage variant
A clean unaltered Korean-customs file used as a functionality control (summarized correctly by all models)
Webpage/document text scraped via Python requests + BeautifulSoup, truncated to 4,000 characters
quantitative_results: 8 models evaluated across 4 injection categories (direct, external/indirect, image, prompt leakage)
Direct injection successful on 6/8 models: GPT-4o, Kimi-K2, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA-3-8B, LLaMA-3-70B, Gemma; resisted by Claude 3 and Mistral-Saba-24B
External/indirect injection successful on 4/8: GPT-4o, Mistral-Saba-24B, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA-3-70B; resisted by Claude 3, Kimi-K2, LLaMA-3-8B, Gemma
Image injection tested on only 2 multimodal models: GPT-4o = success, Claude 3 = partial
Prompt leakage (exfiltration) successful on 5 models: GPT-4o, Claude 3, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA-3-8B, LLaMA-3-70B; Mistral-Saba-24B = partial; Kimi-K2 and Gemma = failure
GPT-4o was the only model compromised in all four categories; Claude 3 was the most resilient (only partial image susceptibility plus a successful leak)
Text truncation fixed at 4,000 visible characters; output token caps of 400 (Claude 3) and 300 (LLaMA-3-8B/70B, Mistral-Saba-24B)
Table 2 maps defenses across 8 pipeline stages
Success rates are categorical (Success/Partial/Failure) only; authors report NO numeric percentages and caveat that figures are partial reconstructions
baselines_compared: Cross-model comparison among the eight LLMs themselves (GPT-4o, Claude 3, Kimi-K2, Mistral-Saba-24B, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA-3-8B, LLaMA-3-70B, Gemma) using each model's built-in safeguards with no supplementary sanitization
No external attack-method baseline or prior-tool comparison; the study measures each model's native robustness rather than benchmarking a new defense against existing ones
